food_subgroup: Festive salad / raw vegetable toss dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Yusheng (prosperity toss salad). 'Salad food product' (FOODON_03311737) is used as the closest parent category. Truffle variant is a premium restaurant adaptation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Yusheng. 'Mixed salad' (A0CKP) is the closest defensible parent. Truffle and vegan variant noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Yusheng or prosperity toss salad. No suitable parent concept identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One shared platter of approximately 600 g, intended for 4 to 6 persons, yielding roughly 100–150 g per person when served at the table.

nutrient_source.title: Yusheng (raw fish salad, mixed vegetables, sauces) — composite estimation from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central component data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for truffle vegan yusheng exists in HPB Foodo or USDA. Values are estimated from HPB Foodo yusheng reference (standard yusheng, ~130 kcal/100g) adjusted for vegan and truffle variant: removal of raw fish reduces protein slightly; truffle oil addition increases fat marginally. Shredded vegetables, plum sauce, and crispy crackers dominate the macronutrient profile. Composite estimation cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for individual components (shredded radish, carrot, pomelo, sesame oil, plum sauce). Confidence: medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable salad with sweet sauce analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for truffle yusheng. GI estimated at 52 based on analogous mixed vegetable salad with sweet plum sauce dressing from University of Sydney GI Database. The dominant glycemic contributors are plum sauce (high sugar) and crispy crackers, offset by high vegetable fibre content and vinegar/acid components in the dressing. Composite GI of approximately 52 (low) is defensible. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 19.5 × 100) / 100 / 100 × 100g portion = approximately 9.9 per 100g reference; for a 150g individual serve: GL ≈ 15.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100g from plum sauce, soy-based seasonings, and crispy crackers
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size as the plum sauce and seasoned crackers contribute significant sodium; a 150g individual serve provides approximately 465 mg sodium, representing about 20% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~52) with notable sugar content (~9.8g/100g) from plum sauce and pomelo
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low-to-moderate GI of approximately 52 and high vegetable fibre content make yusheng a relatively better festive choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes compared to high-GI dishes, but the plum sauce contributes significant free sugars and portion control is advised; individuals should limit sauce quantity and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.7g/100g), no cholesterol (vegan formulation), and beneficial monounsaturated fats from truffle oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The vegan truffle yusheng is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with truffle oil contributing heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids; however, the sodium content from plum sauce and crackers warrants attention for individuals with hypertension-related cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Truffle Prosperity Yusheng (松露捞生) is a premium vegan adaptation of the traditional Singaporean Chinese New Year (CNY) prosperity toss salad, known as Yusheng or Lo Hei. The dish consists of a colourful assembly of finely shredded raw vegetables — including white radish, carrot, cucumber, beetroot, and yam — combined with pomelo segments, pickled ginger, crispy wonton crackers, and toasted sesame seeds, all elevated with the addition of truffle oil and truffle shavings. The dressing typically includes sweet plum sauce, sesame oil, and five-spice powder. The dish is served as a communal platter for 4 to 6 persons, and the act of tossing the ingredients together (lo hei) while reciting auspicious phrases is a beloved CNY ritual symbolising prosperity, good fortune, and abundance. The truffle variant replaces the traditional raw fish (typically salmon) with truffle oil and shavings, making it fully vegan and suitable for plant-based diners. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in calories (~128 kcal/100g), low in saturated fat, cholesterol-free, and provides dietary fibre from the vegetable base. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low), though the plum sauce contributes notable free sugars (~9.8g/100g). Sodium content (~310 mg/100g) is moderate and primarily from the sauce and crackers. The dish is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low-to-moderate GI and fibre content, though sauce quantity should be moderated. It is a festive, culturally significant dish served exclusively during the Chinese New Year season in Singapore and among overseas Chinese communities.
common_user_queries: Vegan Chinese New Year food Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for truffle vegan yusheng exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from HPB Foodo standard yusheng reference adjusted for vegan and truffle variant, cross-referenced with USDA component data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Foodo yusheng reference and USDA FoodData Central component data; no exact match for truffle vegan yusheng exists in any approved primary source. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero by component analysis. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully vegan dish containing no animal products. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; no B12 source present. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as no vitamin D-fortified ingredients are present and no animal sources are used. 6. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable salad with sweet sauce from University of Sydney GI Database; direct measurement not available. 7. Serving size of 600g (shared platter for 4–6 persons) is based on restaurant description; individual portion approximately 100–150g. 8. Sodium content is primarily from plum sauce and seasoned crackers; individuals on sodium-restricted diets should request reduced sauce. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, and other micronutrients are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for shredded vegetables, sesame oil, and pomelo; these are approximate values. 10. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Mixed' as yusheng is a composite dish of vegetables, sauces, and crackers. 11. Cuisine reclassified from 'Global' to 'Singaporean Chinese' as yusheng is a dish of Singaporean Chinese origin.
